speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about how your father was asked to try to get the money back from the Cuban visas.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Cuban immigration permits, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have copies. I have these-- some of these letters. I found them only recently, maybe a couple of years ago, among my mother's papers. My mother only passed away about two and a half years ago, and so I didn't know all the material that she still had, besides what I already had. And these people said we'd pay-- everybody seemed to have paid a different price for these papers. And my-- I have correspondence that I went over last night where my father addressed the Cuban authorities. And there was absolutely no way that anybody was willing to give any kind of money back.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, Mr. Gonzales, he didn't sell them to the people, right? He sold them to somebody else. And all these other people might have disappeared or-- the Cuban government pocketed the money, if nothing else. And, and it was a moot point. There was nothing to be-- nothing to be had, unfortunately, and he couldn't do anything for the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow or other, people were able to scrape together-- perhaps through Jewish organizations, perhaps through private contributions-- the monies to pay for the passage to the United States. I'm not quite sure where our passage money came from. However, we, we left London the end of August. Our ship was leaving from Glasgow, Scotland. While my father was interned, the blitz of London had begun, and I had returned to London to be with my mother. We had raids every day and, specifically, every night for the next three months-- June, July, August. My mother and I ended up in the air-raid shelter every single night. Each-- we lived, at that time, in a rooming house in Willesden Green, which is in the northwest [INAUDIBLE] area of London, a nice neighborhood. The house belonged to a couple of teachers who made it a rooming house. They didn't live there. And each room held a family of refugees, not necessarily-- there were two St. Louis families in this house. There was a Polish opera singer. There were Belgian refugees. It was quite a mixture. And for everything, you had to put money in, in a, in a slot. If you wanted a bath, you put some pennies in. If you wanted heat, if you wanted to cook, everything had to be-- there were little automats to put money in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I know my parents told me they hung out a lot in the library because it was warm in the winter and cool in the summer. They got together with a lot of St. Louis people socially. That was their only entertainment. They surely didn't have money to go to a movie.

speaker: interviewee
text: They bought yesterday bread and yesterday cake for a few pennies. And everybody bought their own cups and saucers because everything was counted out where they lived. And they got together, and in spite of everything, they had a good time until my father was interned. The men were interned, and things got very serious. So every night, we had raids. The city was bombed, and each house had what was called an Anderson shelter. An Anderson shelter consisted of a hole in the ground with a corrugated metal ark cover over it, which was then covered with a lot of dirt. So you-- from the air, you couldn't see what-- there was anything there. And there were a few wooden slats along the walls of this, this dugout area on, on some, some bricks. And that's where we sat. We were dressed for winter because the nights were cool, even in August and in July. And we had about 12 hours' worth of food with us. Every night, this was going on. We went to bed. After a while, the sirens rang. Some people went into the subways, because London's underground was very low, below the ground. But we just went into the shelter. Mostly, the men stayed outside, unless it got serious and the bombing got close. And then they would all jump into the shelter also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we never slept in a bed, for three months, for a whole night, my mother and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of the people that were in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember a family, Heiman, from Cologne, across the hall from us. And they had a daughter, Susan. And of course, the other family from the St. Louis was actually one of the committee gentlemen with his family, a lawyer by the name of Houstopfer, with his wife and daughter, and they lived downstairs. And then there was a Hungarian lady. Her first name was Bergi. And, and I don't remember the Polish opera singer's name or the Irish ladies upstairs, but those are the names I do remember. And also, one of the more humorous aspects was that the opera singer never paid his rent on time. And so regularly, the landlady came and threw his belongings, in a suitcase, down the steps. And he would call the police, and they wouldn't come because they were tired of it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was-- it was a, a comic situation among some very serious business. And meanwhile, my mother ran down the doors of the various offices of the government to try and get my father processed so we could leave. And she-- she managed it, and he was brought to London under guard of Scotland Yard, like a criminal.

speaker: interviewee
text: They saw each other from across the street. I think he managed to slip her a note. And, and then we-- I don't remember the exact date when we then trained for Glasgow, but it was an overnight ride. And, and we were stopped by air raids several times during the night. The train stopped. The rumor was that the men from the internment camps who were scheduled to board with us were in a sealed car at the end of the train, again, under Scotland Yard guard. We got to Glasgow, and they brought us to the ship. The SS Cameronia was the name of the ship-- C-A-M-E-R-O-N-I-A. Who wasn't there was my father and the other men who were expected. We waited and we waited. I think we were supposed to sail toward evening, and finally, maybe an hour before sailing time, the men came on board. What had happened was that they put them on another ship to go to Australia. You see, the British had shipped many young single men of, of military age who were among those internees-- had shipped them off to Australia and New Zealand. And somebody thought that this batch of men were also supposed to be destined, and I think they, they mutinied. And so finally, they appeared on the ship, and that was quite a reunion. The ship left. We had on board with us the entire-- there was only two class-- second class and third class, in this case. Second class was entirely British children who were being sent to this hemisphere for the duration, to the States and to Canada. And we had a convoy that crossed, crossed with us. Luckily, we made it. We were pursued by German submarines. The ships before and after us were, were torpedoed. We got through, luckily. And so we arrived in New York on September 10, 1940. We hadn't seen a city in lights for years because even before-- even when we still lived in Germany, there were demi-blackouts. And there were-- there were air-raid practices long before Germany was in the war. Everybody had to have an air-raid shelter in their house, and we hadn't seen a city lit up-- well, when we first arrived in London, I suppose-- but in this whole year. And here was New York City. Our relatives who provided the affidavit for us, Mr. and Mrs. Kurt Blum of Philadelphia, picked us up at the ship. Mr. Blum's father-in-law, his name was Moses Lieberman. And at the time, a vice president of Snellenburg's department store here was an additional sponsor, since Mr. Blum's sponsorship was not enough, financially, to sustain us. The relationship, officially, was that Mr.-- that my parents were Mr. Blum's aunt and uncle. Basically, not quite, my mother's sister had married into the family of Mr. Blum's mother. And so they were really just in-laws, indirectly, [INAUDIBLE], as we would say. However, when Mr. Blum was still young and his father passed away in Germany, in the '20s, my mother spent a whole year in his house to help his mother get ready to emigrate to the States, because at that time, already, she had several brothers here who wanted her to come over, since she was widowed. This was the Heidelberger family who, for a long period of time, had a chocolate factory here in Philadelphia, Brothers Heidelberger Chocolates. And so it was, perhaps, a favor repaid. And perhaps it was simply because they wanted to help, but it will never stop being grateful to the Blums for helping us to come to this country. If it weren't for them, we wouldn't be here. I wouldn't be here, and my children wouldn't be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we first came here, we came to Philadelphia right away. And I lived with the, the Blums for a whole year because my parents, at the time, had no means of, of keeping me. My mother was a domestic in a household. My father, as a lawyer, certainly couldn't pursue his profession here. At the time we arrived here, my father was 58 years old. He was not in good health, and he started out by peddling European-made candy from door to door. And he developed all kinds of contacts that way. Eventually, he got a job as a translating editor with a German newspaper here in Philadelphia. And that was a very good job for those days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The newspaper was called the And it actually had published here already through World War I and through World War II, until the '50s. I think it then became a weekly, and eventually it merged with a New York German newspaper called the [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know if that's still in existence. But at any rate, my father worked there until he became too ill to work. My father had a problem that nobody seemed to know what it was. He was treated for gallbladder. He was treated for all kinds of theories. He saw big doctors in Europe. He saw big doctors right here in the United States. Nobody could figure out what was wrong. After he died, we had an autopsy taken, simply because we felt he would have wanted that. And it turns out that he had a hiatal hernia that pressed on the heart. For years, he would have terrible pains, like a heart attack. And he would be able to, sometimes, stop them by a pressure. And therefore, he never wanted to have any exploratory surgery. We think that this originated on a toboggan accident that he had in Italy as a bachelor. He used to go to Italy, tobogganing, as a bachelor. And ever since that accident that he had, he had this problem. They treated him for gallbladder at the end, and he lost-- that was a fatless diet, and he lost whatever little strength he had left. And unfortunately, I was only 17 when my father passed away. That was in 1945. I was still in high school. And he told me one day, while we were sitting at breakfast and before I was going to school, that he didn't think he could last much longer, and I should look after my mother. And that's how he sent me to school that day. And Thanksgiving of 1945, he passed away, much too early.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what did you and your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had a job, I mean, and I was still in high school when my father died. I was a, a junior in high school. I had a job after school. I worked as a salesperson in Gimbels, Wednesday nights and Saturdays all day. And I gave my-- all my money to my mother, except, I think, $2. That was my allowance. And my mother worked in a jewelry store for a long time. You know, after that first year when she was a domestic and my father was peddling candy, after that she got a job in a factory. And then once my father got this job in the-- with the newspaper, they were able to get an apartment, and I moved back with them. I had lived with the Blums for one year, and then after three years, I was finally back with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go to school after high School

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to Philadelphia High School for Girls, and then I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right out of high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a German-Jewish club here in Philadelphia called the Central Club. It was a club for Jews from Central Europe. And my husband had been one of the founders, and his brother as well. It was-- at the time that I joined, it was on North Broad Street, near Susquehanna. And we lived on Park Avenue, near-- in that neighborhood, near the old [INAUDIBLE] synagogue. And there was a youth group, and once I was 16 or so, I was of age to join the youth group. I was one of the younger ones. And this was during the war. There were-- the only-- the only young men around were 4-Fs. Everybody else was in the service. Nevertheless, there was a place to go to. There was ping pong to play. There were records to, to listen to and letters to write to the, the boys who were in the service. And when somebody came home on leave, they always came to the club. And my mother-- it was within walking distance of where we lived, and my mother didn't have to worry where I was hanging out. There was a restaurant in the clubhouse where you could have very good and reasonable meals. Often, my family used to eat there Friday nights. And Fridays with my father's day off, and so sometimes he would treat, for dinner, to go out there. And it was a good-- and, and I met young people there, you know, and people who had the same background as myself. My American classmates didn't have a clue. Nobody ever asked, and I never told. I wanted to fit in. But these were all people who had the similar background, the experience of, of persecution in Germany, and of losing family, family whom we later found out, after the war was over, who perished in concentration camps under most dire circumstances, and people who had to start life from scratch, who came here with nothing. The young-- many of the young people didn't have the privilege of going to school anymore. They were teenagers, which included my husband. He was 18 when he came over here. He came in 1937. He brought over his parents and his two brothers and supported them until he went into the service.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Hans Loeb. [LAUGHS] He was a wonderful person, and I met him after he came out of the service. He was with the American Army Air Force. He was stationed in Europe, and he was in Europe, still, during the war. He was in European Theater, still, during the war, toward the end of the war. And he came back, and I met him in the fall of 1946 in the club. We went on a hike, and I came with one guy, and I left with him. And that started a two-months courtship, and then we got engaged. I was still in high school. My mother had gone visiting my cousin in Connecticut. And she said, you see, I can't ever leave you alone. You're going to do something. And after, I graduated in January of '47, and we got married June 1 of '47. And we were married two weeks short of 40 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all that time, you lived in the Philadelphia area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Philadelphia, yes, we had two children, Joan and Joel. And those children are all grown up by now. My daughter, Joan, is married. She is a very versatile person, having been a television producer and director at channel 29, an art director at a newspaper in Missoula, Montana, and finally, a teacher at Quakertown Friends School. And she has a, a son, Asha, my oldest grandson, who is my sunshine. I have a son, Joel. Joel lives in Virginia with his wife, Susan. By the way, Joanie is married to Robert Michener, a, a TV person, completely well-rounded TV person, an engineer. And my son is married to Susan [INAUDIBLE]. They both work for Marriott Corporation. My son is a director of projects for human resources, and my daughter-in-law is in charge of menus. They work at the international headquarters in Bethesda, Maryland, for Marriott. They have two children. The oldest one will be six soon. His name is Benjamin Hans. And the little girl is going to be three in a couple of weeks, and her name is Lexis Halsey Loeb. That's my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to tell us about your life here, your adult life here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my adult life here, I went to art school after high school. I went to Philadelphia College of Art for a short time. I worked at Temple University Library for a while, then I got into the art field, which was my goal. Eventually, after about five years of marriage, I had children. We moved into a house, and I stayed home and raised my children. I was very active in PTA. I was very active on the Jewish scene, here in Philadelphia. I was the president of my sisterhood. We belong to congregation [INAUDIBLE], which was a German-Jewish congregation. My husband was one of the founders. I also-- at the same time, we belong to [INAUDIBLE], which was where I was confirmed, where I was married. And after [INAUDIBLE] closed its doors because of attrition, we were solely [INAUDIBLE]. My children were confirmed there. My son was bar mitzvahed there. I'm still there. I was active with the Women's Movement for Conservative Judaism, Philadelphia branch. And I went back to work when my children were about 11 and 13, and, again, went back into the art field. I went to school, to night school again, and worked first, for-- I was art director for a manufacturing concern for 10 years. I worked as a artist, graphic artist, and German correspondent for an electronic firm. And then my last job was another 10-year stint with a printing house as graphic designer. My husband passed away, unfortunately, over nine years ago. We were just two weeks short of our 40th anniversary. He had a bad heart. He had two open-heart surgeries, and he didn't make it out of the second one, unfortunately. It was my sadness that-- he was already retired at that time-- that, being 10 years younger, I wasn't entitled to be retired yet, and we couldn't play together. And that's something that I've always regretted very much. He busied himself with part-time work in real estate, and after he passed away, that was a very difficult adjustment for me-- I think, more than any other that I've had to make in my life, including the emigration and resettling in various countries. But you do-- you learn to cope. I mean, you know, you cope with everything else, and you learn to cope with that too. My children were extremely supportive. My daughter, at the time, lived in Montana. So that wasn't so close, but my son was close by and a tremendous support for me. And after about two years, I met an old friend whom I had known 40-some years ago. And he was in-- in the meantime, he had lived here. He had lived in Israel. He's a survivor of Auschwitz. He has his own story to tell. And we started to see each other, and we've been together, now, over seven years. At the moment, I'm recovering from surgery, and he is a caretaker beyond description.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Max Percal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Our first visit back to Germany was in 1963 when we had the opportunity of joining a charter trip to Dusseldorf for a printing convention, which takes place there every year, with friends of ours who are in the business. We were the only non-printers on the plane. And at that time, I have to tell you, I was very nervous about returning to Germany. My nanny came to meet us at the plane, and the whole plane knew about this reunion. You know, everybody watched this, when she stood there with a bunch of flowers and we saw each other again. [CRYING] And at that time, I-- my husband took me to his hometown, which was Andernach, a small town on the Rhine near Koblenz. And that was a very tearful experience. It was a very small town. At the time he lived there, there were just 10,000 people, and the Loeb family was very well known. They were in the distilling business, wine and distilling business, liquor distilling business, and they were well known. He had been there as a GI and arrested a few Nazis, which, meanwhile, got out again. And as I said, it was rather a tearful experience. I met a cousin again, in Andernach, who had been an Auschwitz survivor and was on business in Germany from South Africa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met the daughter of the Steinfield family on that occasion also. We talked about them being our benefactors during the pre-war days. And she had survived a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was, then, Anita van Gelder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin was Hainz Hesterfer. For He was my father's sister's son who survived Auschwitz, who wrote his memoirs. And that's how we found out what was going on in the camps. He had a brother and his mother, both, who didn't survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: At that occasion, I went back to my hometown with my husband. And my nanny and her husband accompanied us. I boldly rang the bell at my house, and I said, my name is Liesl Joseph Loeb. I used to live here. Would you allow me to come in and show my husband the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman who had bought the house from my mother was a schoolmate of my mother. And she bought the house, at the time, for peanuts. Later on, she had to pay restitution to my mother, and I think she was-- I mean, she was as pale as a ghost when I told her who I was, and she let us in and, and look at the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I realized that was really not nice of me to-- that was a bombshell. But I didn't want anything from her. I just wanted to see my house again. Somehow or other, that house was the embodiment of the security of my childhood. I don't know how else to explain that, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was always in my mind, that I had to go back and see if it was really there. So that was the first occasion. On a later visit, we took our children. They were then of college age, and my husband, his two brothers, their children, we and our children agreed to meet in Germany, in Andernach, to have an experience with our children.
